children.value: Within this notebook, we will cover:
Calculation of velocity texture using Py-ART

import os
import warnings
import cartopy.crs as ccrs
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import pyart
import fsspec
import icechunk
import xarray as xr

OSN_ENDPOINT = "https://umn1.osn.mghpcc.org"
BUCKET = "nexrad-arco"

warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

jupyter_data.name: stdout
jupyter_data.text:

children.value: Read in the Data and Plot the Data

children.label: Read in the Data and Plot the Data

children.value: Read in a sample file from the Fruska Gora (FGora) radar

children.label: Read in a sample file from the Fruska Gora (FGora) radar

children.value: Our data is formatted as a Rainbow file used by Leonardo radars. We will download the data locally so that Py-ART’s Rainbow reader can read the files.

from pathlib import Path
fs = fsspec.filesystem(
    "s3", anon=True, client_kwargs={"endpoint_url": OSN_ENDPOINT},
)
fgora_raw = sorted(fs.glob(f"{BUCKET}/fgora_vol/**/*.vol"))
download_dir = Path("data/fgora_sample")
download_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

sample_ts = "2014051500012000"
for remote in [f for f in fgora_raw if sample_ts in f]:
    local = download_dir / Path(remote).name
    if not local.exists():
        fs.get(remote, str(local))
    print(f"  {local.name}")
Plot a quick-look of reflectivity and velocity

children.label: Plot a quick-look of reflectivity and velocity

children.value: We can start by taking a quick look at the reflectivity and velocity fields. Notice how the velocity field is
, indicated by the speckles and high/low values directly next to each other

fig = plt.figure(figsize=[8, 10])
ax = plt.subplot(211, projection=ccrs.PlateCarree())
display = pyart.graph.RadarMapDisplay(radar)
display.plot_ppi_map('reflectivity',
                     ax=ax,
                     sweep=0,
                     resolution='50m',
                     vmin=0,
                     vmax=60, 
                     lat_lines=[43, 44, 45, 46, 47],
                     projection=ccrs.PlateCarree(),
                     cmap='HomeyerRainbow')

ax2 = plt.subplot(2,1,2,projection=ccrs.PlateCarree())
display = pyart.graph.RadarMapDisplay(radar)
display.plot_ppi_map('velocity',
                     ax=ax2,
                     sweep=0,
                     resolution='50m',
                     lat_lines=[43, 44, 45, 46, 47],
                     lon_lines=[16, 17, 18, 19, 20, 21, 22, 23],
                     vmin=-10,
                     vmax=10, 
                     projection=ccrs.PlateCarree(),
                     cmap='balance')
plt.show()

The radial velocity measured by the radar is mesasured by detecing the phase shift between the transmitted pulse and the pulse recieved by the radar. However, using this methodology, it is only possible to detect phase shifts within

children.html: ±2π\pm 2\pi±2π

children.value: due to the periodicity of the transmitted wave. Therefore, for example, a phase shift of

children.html: 3π3\pi3π

children.value: would erroneously be detected as a phase shift of

children.html: −π-\pi−π

children.value: and give an incorrect value of velocity when retrieved by the radar. This phenomena is called aliasing. The maximium unambious velocity that can be detected by the radar before aliasing occurs is called the Nyquist velocity.

In the next example, you will see an example of aliasing occurring, where the values of +10 m/s abruptly transition into a region of -10 m/s, with -5 m/s in the middle of the region around 45 N, 21 E.

First, for dealiasing to work efficiently, we need to use a GateFilter. Notice that, this time, the data shown does not give us a nice gate_id. This is what raw data looks like, and we need to do some preprocessing on the data to remove noise and clutter. Thankfully, Py-ART gives us the capability to do this. As a simple filter in this example, we will first calculate the velocity texture using Py-ART’s
function. The velocity texture is the standard deviation of velocity surrounding a gate. This will be higher in the presence of noise.
The name of our velocity field
The number of gates within our window to use to calculate the texture
PyART normally supports the retrieval of the nyquist velocity through its
object. However, the Nyquist velocity is not stored in these files. Therefore, we will have to find the maximum of the velocity field in order to tease out the Nyquist velocity.
While the nyquist velocity is stored as an array, we see that these are all the same value...
Calculate Velocity Texture and Filter our Data

children.label: Calculate Velocity Texture and Filter our Data

children.value: Now that we have an ide?a of which parameters to pass in, let’s calculate velocity texture!

children.value: function results in a data dictionary, including the actual data, as well as metadata. We can add this to our
method, passing the name of our new field (
), and a clarification that we want to replace the existing velocity texture field if it already exists in our radar object (
Now that we have our velocity texture field added to our radar object, let’s plot it!

fig = plt.figure(figsize=[8, 8])
display = pyart.graph.RadarMapDisplay(radar)
display.plot_ppi_map('texture',
                     sweep=0,
                     resolution='50m',
                     vmin=0,
                     vmax=10, 
                     projection=ccrs.PlateCarree(),
                     cmap='balance')
plt.show()
Determine a Suitable Velocity Texture Threshold

children.label: Determine a Suitable Velocity Texture Threshold

children.value: Plot a histogram of velocity texture to get a better idea of what values correspond to hydrometeors and what values of texture correspond to artifacts.
In the below example, a threshold of 3 would eliminate most of the peak corresponding to noise around 6 while preserving most of the values in the peak of ~0.5 corresponding to hydrometeors.

hist, bins = np.histogram(radar.fields['texture']['data'][np.isfinite(radar.fields['texture']['data'])],
                          bins=150)
bins = (bins[1:]+bins[:-1])/2.0

plt.plot(bins,
         hist,
         label='Velocity Texture Frequency')
plt.axvline(3,
            color='r',
            label='Proposed Velocity Texture Threshold')

plt.xlabel('Velocity texture')
plt.ylabel('Count')
plt.legend()

In this example, we only see a unimodal distribution of velocity texture. This is because the Rainbow files already have noise filtering applied by the radar’s internal processing. However, if the default processing does not remove enough noise and clutter, then it can further be refined by following the steps in this notebook.
Setup a Gatefilter Object and Apply our Threshold

children.label: Setup a Gatefilter Object and Apply our Threshold

children.value: Now we can set up our GateFilter (
), which allows us to easily apply masks and filters to our radar object.

would be suitable for this dataset, we use the
method, specifying we want to exclude
Now that we have created a gatefilter, filtering our data using the velocity texture, let’s plot our data!
We need to pass our gatefilter to the
to apply it to our plot.

# Plot our Unfiltered Data
fig = plt.figure(figsize=[8, 10])
ax = plt.subplot(211, projection=ccrs.PlateCarree())
display = pyart.graph.RadarMapDisplay(radar)
display.plot_ppi_map('velocity',
                     title='Raw Radial Velocity (no filter)',
                     ax=ax,
                     sweep=0,
                     resolution='50m',
                     vmin=-17,
                     vmax=17,
                     projection=ccrs.PlateCarree(),
                     colorbar_label='Radial Velocity (m/s)',
                     cmap='balance')

ax2 = plt.subplot(2,1,2,projection=ccrs.PlateCarree())

# Plot our filtered data
display = pyart.graph.RadarMapDisplay(radar)
display.plot_ppi_map('velocity',
                     title='Radial Velocity with Velocity Texture Filter',
                     ax=ax2,
                     sweep=0,
                     resolution='50m',
                     vmin=-17,
                     vmax=17, 
                     projection=ccrs.PlateCarree(),
                     colorbar_label='Radial Velocity (m/s)',
                     gatefilter=gatefilter,
                     cmap='balance')
plt.show()
Dealias the Velocity Using the Region-Based Method

children.label: Dealias the Velocity Using the Region-Based Method

children.value: At this point, we can use the
to dealias the velocities and then add the new field to the radar!

velocity_dealiased = pyart.correct.dealias_region_based(radar,
                                                        vel_field='velocity',
                                                        nyquist_vel=nyquist_velocity,
                                                        centered=True,
                                                        gatefilter=gatefilter)

# Add our data dictionary to the radar object
radar.add_field('corrected_velocity', velocity_dealiased, replace_existing=True)
Plot the new velocities, which now look much more realistic.

fig = plt.figure(figsize=[8, 8])
display = pyart.graph.RadarMapDisplay(radar)
display.plot_ppi_map('corrected_velocity',
                     sweep=0,
                     resolution='50m',
                     vmin=-40,
                     vmax=40, 
                     projection=ccrs.PlateCarree(),
                     colorbar_label='Radial Velocity (m/s)',
                     cmap='balance',
                     gatefilter=gatefilter)
plt.show()
Compare our Raw Velocity Field to our Dealiased, Cleaned Velocity Field

children.label: Compare our Raw Velocity Field to our Dealiased, Cleaned Velocity Field

children.value: As a last comparison, let’s compare our raw, uncorrected velocities with our cleaned velocities, after applying the velocity texture threshold and dealiasing algorithm

# Plot our Unfiltered Data
fig = plt.figure(figsize=[8, 10])
ax = plt.subplot(211, projection=ccrs.PlateCarree())
display = pyart.graph.RadarMapDisplay(radar)
display.plot_ppi_map('velocity',
                     title='Raw Radial Velocity (no filter)',
                     ax=ax,
                     sweep=0,
                     resolution='50m',
                     vmin=-30,
                     vmax=30,
                     projection=ccrs.PlateCarree(),
                     colorbar_label='Radial Velocity (m/s)',
                     cmap='balance')

ax2 = plt.subplot(2,1,2,projection=ccrs.PlateCarree())

# Plot our filtered, dealiased data
display = pyart.graph.RadarMapDisplay(radar)
display.plot_ppi_map('corrected_velocity',
                     title='Radial Velocity with Velocity Texture Filter and Dealiasing',
                     ax=ax2,
                     sweep=0,
                     resolution='50m',
                     vmin=-30,
                     vmax=30, 
                     projection=ccrs.PlateCarree(),
                     gatefilter=gatefilter,
                     colorbar_label='Radial Velocity (m/s)',
                     cmap='balance')
plt.show()
Within this lesson, we walked through how to apply radial velocity corrections to a dataset, filtering based on the velocity texture and using a regional dealiasing algorithm.
In the next few notebooks, we walk through gridding radar data and advanced visualization methods!
